skill.name: web-search
skill.description: Use the inference.sh belt CLI to search the web, extract page content, and answer research questions with Tavily and Exa.
skill.summary: Search the web and extract source content through Tavily and Exa using the belt CLI.
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: The static external-command and weak-cryptography alerts are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, inline app IDs, tables, and documentation links. The confirmed risk is that the skill grants Bash access to the belt CLI and intentionally sends user queries, URLs, and extracted content to external Tavily, Exa, OpenRouter, and inference.sh services.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Can Send User Data to Search Providers
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(belt *) and provides belt app run examples for Tavily, Exa, and OpenRouter. This is expected behavior for web search, but user queries, URLs, and extracted content may leave the local environment and require provider trust.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and multiple belt app run examples are direct evidence of external CLI use. The intent is legitimate web search, so the risk is data exposure rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby Backtick Execution in Markdown
low_findings.description: The reported backtick locations are Markdown fences, inline app identifiers, or example command blocks in SKILL.md. No Ruby file or Ruby shell backtick execution is present in the reviewed skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible in SKILL.md as documentation formatting or shell examples. I found no evidence of Ruby code or dynamic command construction at these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto lines do not contain cryptographic operations. They are the skill description, Markdown table headers, and a documentation link, so the high-severity static alerts should be dismissed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines were reviewed directly and contain no hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage. This appears to be a pattern-match artifact.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill includes hardcoded links to inference.sh documentation, a hosted image, example.com sample URLs, and related articles. These links support documentation and examples, but they create expected third-party network touchpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly present in SKILL.md and align with the web-search purpose. I did not find evidence that these links exfiltrate secrets or bypass user intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard Belt Bash Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed tool scope permits Bash(belt *), which can run any belt subcommand available to the agent. This broad scope is functional for the skill but should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard permission is explicit in the skill front matter. The impact depends on belt configuration and available authenticated apps.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Web Search and Extraction Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples run Tavily and Exa search or extraction apps through belt. These commands require network access and can transmit user-provided queries and URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples clearly call third-party search and extraction apps. This is intended functionality, but it is a concrete privacy and network dependency risk.

content.value_statement: Current web research is difficult when answers need sources and extracted page content. This skill uses Tavily and Exa through the belt CLI to gather links, answers, and clean text for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Runs Tavily search-assistant through belt for source-backed web answers.
Runs Tavily extract to collect clean text and images from provided URLs.
Runs Exa search for relevant web links with context.
Runs Exa answer for direct factual responses.
Runs Exa extract to retrieve and analyze page content.
Shows workflows that combine search or extraction with Claude models.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an authenticated account.
Depends on Tavily, Exa, OpenRouter, and inference.sh service availability.
May send queries, URLs, and extracted content to external providers.
Does not validate source accuracy beyond the provider results.

use_cases.title: Source-backed topic research
use_cases.description: Gather current sources and short answers before writing a report, brief, or analysis.

use_cases.title: RAG source collection
use_cases.description: Find and extract web pages that can be reviewed before adding them to retrieval workflows.

use_cases.title: Claim verification
use_cases.description: Search for supporting sources and extract referenced pages before checking factual claims.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Web Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the web for recent information about this topic and return the most relevant sources with a short summary: [topic].

prompt_templates.title: Extract Article Content
prompt_templates.scenario: Retrieve clean content from known URLs
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the main content from these URLs and summarize the useful facts: [URLs].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Search Providers
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate coverage from Tavily and Exa
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Tavily and Exa to research [topic]. Compare the strongest sources, note disagreements, and list gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Build Research Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for authoritative sources about [domain question], extract the best pages, and prepare a concise source pack for later analysis.

output_examples.input: Research current best practices for AI agent evaluation.
output_examples.output: A concise answer with several source links, short notes on each source, and a list of practices to verify before use.

output_examples.input: Extract the main content from three product documentation pages.
output_examples.output: Clean page summaries with important headings, useful details, and image references when the provider returns them.

output_examples.input: Find sources for a claim about a recent technology trend.
output_examples.output: A source-backed verification summary that separates confirmed points, uncertain points, and missing evidence.

best_practices: Review source URLs before using results in final work.
Avoid sending secrets, private documents, or sensitive customer data in queries.
Use extraction on specific URLs when accuracy matters more than broad discovery.
anti_patterns: Do not treat provider answers as verified facts without checking sources.
Do not use broad queries when a precise source list is available.
Do not run extraction on private or restricted pages without permission.

faq.question: What services does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses the inference.sh belt CLI with Tavily, Exa, and optional OpenRouter model workflows.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Yes. Web search and extraction require requests to external providers.

faq.question: Can it extract content from any URL?
faq.answer: It can request extraction for provided URLs, but access depends on provider limits and page availability.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Queries, URLs, and extracted content may be sent to third-party services.

faq.question: Does it replace manual fact-checking?
faq.answer: No. It helps collect sources, but users should verify important claims independently.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
